5 Key Takeaways from Control 2025 (Trends and Innovations from Europe’s Big Metrology Show)

09 May 2025 ·   5 min read

Control 2025 in Stuttgart brought together the world’s top metrology innovators and quality professionals. As a leading exhibitor, API Metrology showcased our latest measurement systems and observed several strong industry trends that reflect where precision manufacturing is headed. From artificial intelligence and automation to new optical scanning techniques, the Control 2025 metrology event highlighted how quality assurance technology is evolving. Below we outline five key takeaways from Europe’s big metrology show, illustrating the themes poised to shape the future of precision measurement.

Takeaway #1: Artificial Intelligence Powers Smarter, Faster Inspection

One of the most prominent themes at Control 2025 was the surge of artificial intelligence in inspection solutions. AI-powered systems for defect recognition, predictive maintenance, and real-time inspection optimization were front-and-center in many exhibits. Attendees saw machine learning algorithms embedded in vision systems that can automatically detect surface defects or dimensional deviations far faster than traditional methods. This kind of automated defect recognition, often using deep learning, promises to catch quality issues that human inspectors might miss, improving accuracy and consistency. API has applied similar AI techniques to enhance the vision camera system in our new iLT laser tracker, enabling faster and more reliable SMR recognition and lock-on in dynamic measurement environments.

Takeaway #2: Automation and Smart Manufacturing Drive Seamless Quality Integration

Many exhibitors at Control 2025 underscored the integration of metrology into fully automated production environments. The emphasis on automation and smart manufacturing was evident in robotic measurement cells, inline inspection stations, and other smart factory demonstrations. The trend is clearly moving towards full automation of inspection processes during production, with quality checks that run inline, faster and more efficiently, integrated into diverse production sequences.

Instead of quality control being a separate, manual step in a lab, it is increasingly built into the manufacturing line. We saw coordinate measuring machines (CMMs) tended by robots, conveyor systems with vision inspection checkpoints, and robot arms equipped with 3D scanners measuring parts in real time. These examples highlight broader metrology automation trends across the industry, aiming to make quality assurance a seamless part of production (often referred to as “Quality 5.0”).

Closing the loop is a major goal of this automation push. Many solutions featured at Control 2025 enable measurement data to feed directly into process control systems. For instance, if our 9D LADAR, an inline laser scanner detects a part drifting out of tolerance, it can instantly signal the production machine to adjust its settings. By integrating metrology with manufacturing execution systems, companies can achieve true closed-loop control where machines self-correct based on inspection feedback. This level of connectivity transforms quality control from a reactive process into a proactive one.

Takeaway #3: Optical and Non-Contact Technologies Redefine Precision Scanning

Another clear highlight was the wave of breakthroughs in non-contact and optical measurement technologies. Across the exhibition halls, companies showed off advanced 3D scanning systems, optical sensors, and laser-based metrology devices that can inspect parts without physical contact. From structured light scanners to interferometry-based systems, these non-contact inspection systems are achieving higher speeds and accuracies than ever before.

The push for ultra-fast, high-precision scanning was palpable – several demos featured sensors capturing dense point clouds of complex parts within seconds, enabling 100% inspection of components where sampling used to be the norm.

API also contributed to this non-contact momentum with our latest products. Our Dynamic 9D LADAR system, for example, reflects the industry’s move toward high-speed, laser-based scanning. LADAR (Laser Detection and Ranging) is a revolutionary non-contact technology that uses advanced optics to capture detailed dimensional data rapidly and with micron-level precision. A live demo at the API booth showed how the 9D LADAR can scan large aerospace components in minutes, detecting subtle surface deviations without ever touching the part.

These examples underscore the broader trend: manufacturers are embracing lasers, optics, and other non-contact methods to perform faster and more flexible inspections. The consensus from Control 2025 is that the future of metrology is increasingly optical – shining light on parts to measure them precisely, rather than probing them mechanically.

Takeaway #4: Digital Twins and Connected Data Ecosystems Transform Quality Insight

Also highlighted the expansion of digital twin technology and data connectivity in metrology. Many exhibitors focused on syncing measurement systems with factory software – from connecting portable metrology devices to Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES) and Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) platforms, to integrating measurement results into simulation and CAD software.

This trend of digital thread connectivity means quality data no longer lives in isolation. Instead, inspection results feed directly into the broader manufacturing data ecosystem, enabling real-time analysis and decision-making. For example, some vendors showed dashboards where CMM results and laser scanner point clouds were automatically sent to a central quality database, updating yield metrics and triggering alerts if any dimensions went out of spec. By linking metrology with these IT systems, companies can break down data silos and respond faster to issues.

Takeaway #5: Compact and Portable Metrology Tools Enable On-the-Go Precision

The final major theme from Control 2025 was the strong demand for compact and portable metrology solutions. Not every shop has the space or budget for a large fixed CMM, and the industry is responding with smaller-footprint tools that can be easily moved to the point of need. On the show floor, there were numerous portable arms, handheld 3D scanners, and laser trackers being demonstrated – all designed to bring high-precision measurement capability directly to the shop floor or even on-site at remote locations.

Likewise, our new API iLT laser tracker – the world’s most compact laser tracker – demonstrates how portable optical metrology is achieving lab-grade accuracy on the shop floor. By combining an integrated laser system with high-accuracy tracking, the iLT can measure large assemblies with a unit small enough to carry anywhere

This continuing trend toward portability is driven by clear benefits: the ability to bring metrology equipment to the production line (or the field) eliminates the need to transport parts to a fixed CMM, reducing downtime and accelerating workflows. Manufacturers love these mobile solutions because they introduce flexibility; quality checks can happen wherever the part is, whether it’s a large aerospace assembly that can’t be easily moved or a machine on the factory floor that needs alignment verification.

At the event, we saw improvements in portability without sacrificing accuracy. New articulated arm CMMs are lighter and more ergonomic, some even fold for easy storage. Handheld laser scanners are getting more powerful yet remain user-friendly, allowing operators to capture millions of points on a part with a device they can carry around. Laser trackers, traditionally somewhat bulky, have dramatically shrunk in size – for example, one latest-generation tracker on display (API’s own iLT) weighs only ~5 kg but delivers the high precision expected from a lab instrument.

Conclusion:

Control 2025 provided an exciting glimpse into the future of metrology and quality assurance. From AI-driven inspection and fully automated quality loops to cutting-edge non-contact systems and on-the-go measurement tools, the show’s trends confirm that our industry is innovating on all fronts. API Metrology was proud to be part of this event and to demonstrate how our solutions align with these developments. The Control 2025 metrology trends outlined above all point toward more intelligent, integrated, and efficient approaches to ensuring manufacturing quality. If you missed us at the show, we encourage you to reach out for a personal demonstration or watch our recap video from the event. As we review the key takeaways and feedback from Stuttgart, API will continue pushing the envelope in precision measurement – and we look forward to helping you apply these innovations to advance your own quality processes. Here’s to the road ahead in metrology, building on the momentum from Control 2025!

# Metrology 4.0 – Shaping the Future of Measurement

The advent of Industry 4.0, characterized by smart factories and cyber-physical systems, has ushered in a new era for various industrial domains, including metrology. Metrology 4.0, an extension of this industrial revolution, represents the integration of advanced technologies including The Internet of Things, Artificial Intelligence , and Big Data into measurement science. This integration is reshaping manufacturing processes, enhancing quality, and paving the way for innovative developments in metrology equipment.

#### **Understanding Metrology 4.0**

Metrology 4.0 is the application of digital technologies to measurement. Traditional metrology relied heavily on manual and mechanical methods, which, while precise, lack the agility and connectivity demanded by modern manufacturing. Metrology 4.0, however, leverages digital tools to provide real-time, highly accurate measurements and seamless data integration within manufacturing ecosystems.

Key components of Metrology 4.0 include:

**Internet of Things (IoT):** IoT-enabled devices can communicate and exchange data autonomously, providing continuous monitoring and feedback in real-time.

**Artificial Intelligence (AI):** AI algorithms analyze measurement data to predict trends, detect anomalies, and optimize processes.

**Big Data Analytics:** The vast amount of data generated by modern metrology equipment is processed to extract actionable insights, improving decision-making.

**Cloud Computing:** Cloud platforms facilitate the storage and sharing of measurement data, ensuring accessibility and collaboration across different locations.

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS):** Integration of physical measurement devices with digital systems to create intelligent, self-regulating environments.

#### **The Impact of Metrology 4.0 on Manufacturing**

The integration of Metrology 4.0 into manufacturing processes wil revolutionize manufacturing industry in several key ways:

**Enhanced Precision and Accuracy:** Metrology 4.0 ensures unparalleled precision and accuracy in measurements, which is critical for maintaining product quality and meeting stringent industry standards. The use of advanced sensors, real-time data processing, and AI-driven analytics enables manufacturers to achieve and sustain high levels of accuracy, minimizing defects and rework.

**Real-Time Monitoring and Control:** With IoT and CPS, manufacturers can achieve real-time monitoring and control of their production processes. Sensors embedded in machinery and products continuously collect data, which is analyzed instantly. This real-time feedback loop allows for immediate adjustments, ensuring processes remain within optimal parameters and reducing downtime.

**Predictive Maintenance:** Predictive maintenance, powered by AI and big data analytics, is a significant benefit of Metrology 4.0. By analyzing data trends and patterns, AI can predict potential equipment failures before they occur, allowing for timely maintenance and reducing unplanned downtime. This proactive approach increases equipment lifespan and operational efficiency.

**Improved Supply Chain Integration:** Metrology 4.0 facilitates seamless integration across the supply chain. Measurement data can be shared in real-time with suppliers and customers, enhancing transparency and collaboration. This integration ensures that quality standards are maintained throughout the supply chain, reducing variability and improving overall product quality.

**Enhanced Customization and Flexibility:** The flexibility offered by Metrology 4.0 allows manufacturers to adapt quickly to changing market demands. Customization of products to meet specific customer requirements becomes more feasible with precise, real-time measurements and adaptive manufacturing processes. This agility helps manufacturers stay competitive in a rapidly evolving market.

#### **Influencing Future Development of Metrology Equipment**

The transformative impact of Metrology 4.0 on manufacturing is driving significant advancements in metrology equipment. The future development of these tools is being shaped by several key trends:

**Smart and Connected Devices:** Future metrology equipment will be increasingly smart and connected. IoT-enabled devices will become standard, allowing for continuous data collection, real-time monitoring, and seamless integration with other systems. These smart devices will be capable of self-calibration, reducing the need for manual intervention and ensuring consistent accuracy.

**Advanced Sensors and Miniaturization:** Advances in sensor technology and miniaturization will enable the development of more compact, precise, and versatile measurement tools. These sensors will be capable of operating in harsh environments and measuring a wider range of parameters with greater accuracy.

**Integration with AI and Machine Learning:** AI and machine learning will play a crucial role in the evolution of metrology equipment. These technologies will enable predictive analytics, automated decision-making, and adaptive control, further enhancing the precision and efficiency of measurement processes. Equipment will become more autonomous, capable of learning from data and improving over time.

#### **Enhanced User Interfaces and Augmented Reality**

The user interfaces of future metrology equipment will be more intuitive and user-friendly, incorporating augmented reality (AR) for enhanced visualization and interaction. AR can provide technicians with real-time guidance and overlay measurement data directly onto physical objects, improving accuracy and reducing the potential for human error.

**Cloud-Based Platforms and Data Analytics:** Cloud-based platforms will facilitate the storage, sharing, and analysis of measurement data. These platforms will support collaboration across different locations, enabling remote monitoring and control. Advanced data analytics will provide deeper insights into measurement data, helping manufacturers optimize their processes and improve quality.

#### **Metrology 4.0 Is a Transformative Force**

While Metrology 4.0 offers numerous benefits, its implementation is not without challenges. Manufacturers must address several considerations to fully leverage its potential The limited standardization and interoperability between different metrology systems can hinder seamless integration. Transitioning to Metrology 4.0 requires a cultural shift within organizations. Change management strategies must be in place to address resistance and ensure smooth adoption of new technologies and processes.

Metrology 4.0 is revolutionizing manufacturing measurement by integrating advanced technologies which are enhancing precision, efficiency driving significant improvements in quality control and productivity. The ongoing evolution of metrology equipment, shaped by these innovations, will continue to empower manufacturers to meet the challenges of a rapidly changing industry. The future of manufacturing measurement is bright, characterized by smarter, more connected, and more sustainable solutions that redefine the standards of accuracy and efficiency. Metrology 4.0 is not just a technological advancement; it is a transformative force that is redefining the future of manufacturing and measurement.

## For people, place, prosperity and planet, we deliver impact with measurement science

![Delivering impact](/getmedia/bbbe1edc-284e-465d-af49-43d10e4c4820/Delivering-Impact-Image-for-Flyout.jpg.aspx?width=530&height=300&ext=.jpg&ext=.jpg)

# The future of metrology

## Accurate measurement will enable future technology

The role of metrology, the science of measurement, is to ensure that measurements are useful and accurate. It is about more than the routine making of measurements, it concerns the infrastructure that ensures we have confidence in the accuracy of the measurement or the adoption of technology.

NPL, the UK’s National Metrology Institute, works to provide the measurement infrastructure that meets the needs of industry and society. Whether it is improving trust in scientific reporting, reducing waste in manufacturing or accelerating technological progress, metrology is the common theme that underpins our work.

The International System of Units (‘the SI’) is the only globally agreed system of measurement units. The SI defines units for many types of measurement and is necessary to ensure that our everyday measurements remain comparable and consistent worldwide. Since 2020 the seven SI base units have been defined in terms of ‘constants of nature’ which do not change, allowing future improvements in the accuracy and stability of our measurements.

Technology and Measurement Foresighting explored the implications of new technologies and trends on the future of measurement.

[Contact us](mailto:foresighting@npl.co.uk?body=**Message%20from%20Foresighting%20website%20page**)

[Technology and Measurement Foresighting booklet](/resources/technology-and-measurement-foresighting/technology-measurement-brochure)

### Metrology in the 2030s will have three key priorities

#### **Metrology will support a digitally enabled global measurement infrastructure**

#### **Metrology will improve understanding of complex systems**

#### **Metrology will give confidence in decision making**

## What does this mean in practice?

### A digitally enabled measurement infrastructure

The definitions of the SI base units are now designed to enable easier access for end users. New digital knowledge management and dissemination tools will bring primary standards closer to the end of the measurement chain and deliver lower measurement uncertainty for end user.

Digital and machine-readable calibration certificates held on global distributed ledgers will enable *in-situ* calibration of sensors and local standards, improving the provenance of measurements and simplifying traceability chains.

### Understanding of complex systems

Understanding complex systems is becoming more important as we try and manage and control climate change, biological processes and our ever growing, interconnected and interdependent infrastructure.

In order to develop and operate large, multi-scale and multi-level models of complex systems, we will need data quality frameworks to combine multimodal and multi-scale data.

By using a ‘metrology mindset’, complex systems can be analysed and uncertainties assigned, even though many of the measurements involved sit outside the SI.

### Confidence in decision making

Confidence in decision-making will be reliant on the dynamic propagation of uncertainty through computationally intensive models or algorithms**.**

Taking advantage of increased measurement sensitivity, the seamless and secure sharing of data and increased computation power, needs to be combined with a sound metrological framework and understanding. This will lead to better decision making and the application of artificial intelligence and machine learning to new areas, therefore accelerating the process of discovery.

## NPL is setting the agenda for metrology

**Supporting the SI** - NPL is keen to exploit the fact that definitions of the base units are now formulated in a way that allows us to turn future advances in technology directly into improvements in the accuracy of measurements. This allows more end users to have direct access to the benefits of the new definitions.

**The future of the SI** - The revised SI future ensures that we are ready for all future technological and scientific advances such as 5G networks, quantum technologies and other innovations that we are yet to imagine. NPL is actively working on[SI unit research](/resources/the-si-units/current-research-on-the-si-units)  to reduce uncertainty of measurements.

**Digital enablement** - NPL was part of the EMPIR SmartCom project which looked at the communication and validation of smart data in IoT-networks  reliable interoperability is. We are also involved in the CIPM Task Group on the Digital SI and supporting advances in machine learning. 

**Complex systems** - An example of addressing complex systems would be the work we are leading on climate and earth observation via the European Metrology Network.

NPL is leading several [EMPIR projects](http://empir.npl.co.uk/) that are addressing future requirements of the SI and technology.

Embracing the Future: Key Trends Shaping Metrology
==================================================

Metrology is no longer just about precision—it’s about driving innovation and efficiency in industries worldwide.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By [Iris Poblete](https://www.qualitymag.com/authors/3697-iris-poblete)

As industries continue to push boundaries in manufacturing and technology, metrology—the science of measurement—has become a cornerstone of innovation. The need for precision and efficiency has never been greater, and metrology is evolving to meet these demands. Here, we explore some of the most significant trends shaping the field and their impact on [modern quality control](https://www.qualitymag.com/articles/98430-beyond-dmaic-leveraging-ai-and-quality-40-for-manufacturing-innovation-in-the-fourth-industrial-revolution).

### 1. Digital Transformation: Connecting Data

Metrology is undergoing a digital revolution. The Internet of Things (IoT) has brought a new level of connectivity to measurement tools, allowing devices to communicate seamlessly with each other and broader systems. This interconnectivity enables real-time data sharing and monitoring, creating more efficient workflows.

Cloud computing has further enhanced this transformation by allowing data to be stored, analyzed, and accessed from virtually anywhere. For metrology professionals, this means faster decision-making and better collaboration across global teams. Adding artificial intelligence (AI) to the mix allows for advanced data analysis, identifying patterns and predicting potential issues before they arise. Together, these technologies are making metrology more dynamic and responsive than ever before.

### 2. Automation: Smarter, Faster, Better

Automation has become a game-changer in metrology, improving efficiency while minimizing human error. Advanced coordinate measuring machines (CMMs), now equipped with robotic arms, can perform precise and consistent measurements without the need for constant supervision.

*   [New Operator Productivity: Using Predictive Quality to Solve Workforce Challenges](https://www.qualitymag.com/articles/98401-new-operator-productivity-using-predictive-quality-to-solve-workforce-challenges)

*   [We Need Visionary Leadership to Mitigate Climate Change](https://www.qualitymag.com/articles/98394-we-need-visionary-leadership-to-mitigate-climate-change)

Inline metrology systems, integrated directly into production lines, allow for real-time quality control, ensuring that errors are caught and corrected immediately. [Collaborative robots, or cobots](https://www.qualitymag.com/articles/98446-your-road-map-to-pre-engineered-cobot-systems), are also becoming increasingly common, working alongside human operators to perform repetitive tasks with precision. These innovations are not just saving time—they’re setting new standards for consistency and reliability.

### 3. Noncontact Measurement: Precise and Safe

Noncontact measurement techniques are growing in popularity, particularly for industries that deal with delicate or intricate components. Optical metrology, including laser scanning and structured light, offers highly detailed 3D data without risking damage to the part being measured.

Computed tomography (CT) scanning has become a go-to method for inspecting both internal and external features, providing unparalleled insights into hidden defects and intricate structures. Similarly, advancements in surface metrology tools, such as interferometers, are enabling more precise surface analysis. Noncontact methods are proving indispensable for sectors like aerospace, automotive, and medical devices, where precision is paramount.

### 4. Sustainability: Doing More with Less

Sustainability is no longer a buzzword—it’s a necessity. Metrology is playing a key role in helping industries achieve their sustainability goals. Energy-efficient measurement tools are reducing power consumption, while precision measurement minimizes waste by ensuring parts are manufactured correctly on the first try.

Lifecycle analysis is another area where metrology is making an impact. By assessing the durability and performance of products, manufacturers can create more sustainable designs that last longer and reduce the need for replacements. As industries continue to prioritize sustainability, metrology is proving to be an essential ally.

### 5. Portability: Measurements Anywhere

Portability has become a major focus in metrology, enabling on-site measurements and improving flexibility. Handheld 3D scanners are increasingly popular, offering high-accuracy results that can be achieved anywhere—whether on the factory floor or in the field.

Mobile CMMs are also gaining traction, allowing large components to be measured in place, rather than requiring costly transportation to a lab. Wireless solutions further enhance portability by eliminating cables, making setup faster and more convenient. These advancements are empowering manufacturers to conduct quality checks wherever and whenever they’re needed.

### 6. Standards and Certification: Ensuring Consistency

As metrology becomes more complex, maintaining consistency and reliability is crucial. International standards such as [ISO/IEC 17025](https://www.qualitymag.com/articles/98235-how-to-read-and-interpret-iso-iec-17025-calibration-certificates) ensure that measurement systems and processes meet stringent quality requirements.

At the same time, certifications for metrology professionals are becoming increasingly valuable, allowing experts to demonstrate their proficiency with new technologies. These standards and certifications ensure that metrology continues to deliver the reliability and accuracy industries rely on.

### 7. People Still Matter: The Human Touch

While technology is advancing rapidly, the role of skilled professionals remains irreplaceable. Automation and AI are powerful tools, but they can’t replicate the nuanced decision-making and critical thinking that humans bring to the table.

Metrology experts play a vital role in interpreting complex data and addressing unique challenges that require more than just machine precision. Continuous training and professional development are essential to ensure these professionals stay ahead of the curve, blending human expertise with technological innovation.

Metrology is no longer just about precision—it’s about driving innovation and efficiency in industries worldwide. The trends shaping the field today, from digital transformation and automation to sustainability and portability, are paving the way for a smarter, more connected future.

As metrology continues to evolve, it will remain a critical force in helping industries adapt, innovate, and thrive. Its role in ensuring quality and precision will only grow, making it a vital component of the manufacturing landscape for years to come.
